summary: Joel Spolsky addresses early criticism of Stack Overflow by drawing a parallel to Wikipedia's early days. Critics who point out wrong answers are making the same mistake journalists made when criticizing Wikipedia articles that were already fixed by the time their columns went to print. He emphasizes that Stack Overflow is fundamentally a wiki where content improves over time through voting and editing. With 81% of traffic coming from search engines, the real value is in long-term answer quality, not initial responses.
key_points: Stack Overflow reached 8.3 million page views per month within three months of launch
Criticism of Stack Overflow mirrors early Wikipedia criticism: pointing out errors that are already being corrected by the community
81% of Stack Overflow traffic comes from search engines, meaning most value is delivered long after questions are first asked
The wiki model means answers improve over time through editing and voting, making point-in-time criticism misleading
money_quotes: Most of the criticism I've heard of Stack Overflow reminds me of the early criticism of Wikipedia: 'I went to this article and it was wrong.' By the time you read the criticism, the article has been fixed.
By the time their column appeared in print, the Wikipedia article was corrected, making a liar out of the journalist.
Most topics get most of their traffic not in the first few days, but by the Google traffic that comes in for people searching for the same exact problem.
Answers DO get better. If they don't, it's a wiki: fix them.
Instead of complaining about good answers with few votes, vote down the top answer and vote up the better answer.

My criterion for whether Stack Overflow works: when you type your question into Google, and you're happy to see a Stack Overflow result rather than a result at another one of those Q&A sites where you have to sign up and pay a monthly fee to see the answer.
key_insight: Stack Overflow's value should be judged not by the accuracy of initial answers but by the long-term quality of content that improves through wiki-style community editing and voting.
